Okay, we're into step 11, which is your sales funnel. This is an important part of sitting down to write any kind of sales message because you have to understand the nature of the beast. What kind of campaign are you going to run? Before you sit down to write, figure out the funnel that is going to be the path that your prospect takes. When they come into your world, they're going to go through a certain path, they're going to have certain decision points to make before they reach the point where you ask them to buy, or leave their email address, or join your club, or whatever the action is that you want. Sometimes there are evergreen funnels, which are launches that just run steadily and bring people in, much like launches do. Or there's a real launch, which is a lot of buildup and PR, and then an explosive few days where things are available, and then it's not available again after that. Sometimes you have a joint venture where you're in a project where you're going to mail to your list, another guy is going to mail to his list, or you may be cross-pollinating lists, a lot of different things, which makes for different kinds of material that you're going to write for a different process that the prospect is going to go through, a different adventure, if you will. Sometimes with affiliate programs, you're actually taking someone else's product and you're selling it for them to your list. Sometimes all you're doing is lead generation, you're not selling at all. So the decision points are very, very different from a funnel that has a final sale that happens. Sometimes there are multi-step or single sales, is the decision to make. Do you have one item that's for sale and then you're done? Do you have something that you can offer a free option, or they can buy something, or they can buy the better version for more, or they can buy quadruple versions for more. They can get on a renewal system where they keep getting it monthly, and then are there backends once they buy? Is there something else that's offered? What happens to them after the sale? Is there a membership site and then that's a whole other little adventure they're put into. You have to know where this is going. What happens to the prospect if he decides to come into your world, starting with the very first thing he reads or hears or sees from you? What are you trying to accomplish? Always write copy with a goal in mind. What do you want your prospect to do? What do you want your prospect to do? Do you want to opt in? Do you want to buy? Do you want to join? Go to another page, come back? What? Decide before you sit down to write. Now there are a bunch of different delivery systems too, and that will affect how you write. In email, you can't have an endless subject line because it won't appear in their inbox. So there are certain parameters to that. Long, ponderous emails full of video and all kinds of other attachments and stuff can get snagged by spam filters or just plain not get delivered or not load. Video sales letters have their own needs. If your video takes a long time to load, then that's something you have to take into account. Websites, if a website is the only way you're going to reach your prospect, they have to have access to the website. You have to reach them at a time when they're near the computer or their mobile device. Direct mail. If you're going to write in direct mail, there are weight limits because the postage goes up. If you're mailing a lot of letters and you're over an ounce for the letter, then you have to pay a lot more postage and your price of sending this out goes way up. Print ads. You have to meet deadlines. You have to meet the requirements of the magazine and you have to have it there in time for them to run it in their magazine. And there's a limit. Once you've got the print as small as it can go, it's still be readable and that's all it's going to fit on the page. You can't add anything else. You have to know that it's very limited space that you're working with. Broadcast media. There are certain things you can say and not say, show and not show. And on broadcast, there's going to be visuals involved which changes the way your message is presented. In PR, there's all kinds of different ways that public relations is affected by laws and by the way things are done in certain industries. Podcasts have their own rules. If you bring people in through your YouTube channel, there's a whole bunch of rules about that. Do you accept comments? Do you not accept comments? What happens after they sign up? What's going on? And what kind of contact do you actually have with them? Do you renew it every time when you have a new video up or do you have a regular video that they watch and then they become part of the tribe and then they never see it again? Social media has its own rules and boy is it changing fast in social media. So know the nature of the delivery beast. What are the limits and demands of the technology? Video loading time, spam filtering for emails, mobile device compatibility, haiku specifications for AdWords, meaning there's only a certain number of characters you can use and if you go over then you can't. You can't run the ad with a logger. There's no negotiating with the robots running AdWords. So you have to be able to write to their specifications. Obscure SEO blunders, search engine optimization. There are things, mistakes you can make which will send you to the bottom of the page. There are fluctuating social media, no-no's, etc. So know the nature of the delivery beast because it will affect what you write. Also, what are the expectations of your prospect for time, length, attitude, say in your video? Is he used to long videos? Is he expecting a long video? Is he expecting a short video? Does he think he's going to have to spend a lot of time if he decides to go ahead and read or follow through on what you want him to do? Or is he expecting that it will be very, very quick? And what is the attitude that he's expecting? Now you want to surprise him sometimes but you don't want to surprise him to the point that he leaves. And what are the costs and rules of each decision? Well, to go back to that, the costs and rules. Sometimes you will make a decision that has higher refunds come in. And that may be acceptable because your overall profit from the deal may overshadow the fact that you've had a higher percentage of refunds. That actually is a marketing decision you might be able to make. But you have to know what the costs are of doing something. If you want to write a longer direct mail piece that's going to cost more in postage, how does that cost affect the budget? How does that affect whether or not what you mailed out is going to be a success or not? Sometimes people deal on very small margins. And if you have to buy space say on a late night TV show and you're buying space for your little mini advertisement, if you negotiate a price below a certain amount and you bring in the same amount of money, if you've negotiated a price for that ad below that, you've made a profit. If you've negotiated a price that they wanted that's above that, then it's a bomb. So the difference between a winner and a loser can be relative to how you negotiated the upfront costs in getting the same amount of response. So push versus pull marketing is very, very important. You want to, for example, go where he's hanging out or you want to be where he's searching online. So you have to understand the dance you're in with your prospect. He's either looking for what you offer, which we call pull marketing, or you're informing him of opportunities he didn't previously know about, which would be push. So you're either pulling or you're pushing and that affects how you phrase certain things, how you write, the whole attitude you have. Consider the flowchart of your funnel when, where prospects have decision points. When do they, if they say no, what happens to them? If they say yes at this point, what happens to them? If they buy, what happens to them? If they don't buy, what happens to them? You have to understand that. All of this leads, of course, to your iPopping offer where we will get into this, but it's value, value, value, even if you're not selling at this point, even if you're just offering a free newsletter or you're offering some type of freebie or something that they can sign up for. You want to talk about the bargain hunters ridiculously more than fair price. People really like bargains. A lot of times that will flip people when they want it. They're not quite ready to go, but if you make it sure that they understand this is a true, honest bargain, that will sometimes flip the sale right there. Now you need rational reason why stories to, so they can tell the doubters in their lives. I talked about their doubting spouses, their neighbor who always seems to get a better deal than they do, and their doubting brother-in-law who always is looking for a reason to show them up. Give them the reason why they bought. Give them plenty of ammo to be able to come back to these people with rational explanations of why this was a good buy and why they loved it. Also, we call it mooch-pleasing bonuses. Mooch is just a word from the old direct mail days where people would opt in just to get the freebies. But you want that. You want to have a few people just get the free reports and videos because that will also bring in the people who eventually become buyers. A lot of times the free reports and videos that people give out in their marketing are just proof. They're just proof that you know what you're talking about. They're creating the idea that, well, this was a good deal. So what happens when I actually buy from the guy? And then that, of course, must be a better and more pleasing situation. And, of course, guarantees which we'll get into. We want to calm the paranoia in the prospect. So that is an important part, but it was a pretty quick little thing to go through. And I hope you got a lot out of that. Okay, sales funnels. Obviously, on its own, it's its own area of study, but we thought it was important to put some framing around the copy that just get you to think about how you're going to use the copy that you're right. And to start as you're putting words together, they just for purpose. So we just want to go over, if not so much questions, some things you should be thinking about or some pitfalls we've seen people go through the silk writing system. The copy is not the problem. Let's say how the plan is the problem. So let's talk a little bit about you lay out your funnel. And if someone lays out a funnel that is really dependent upon the prospect consuming all the content, really reading to the last page of that free report. Or watching all 97 minutes of the free videos. Imagine your funnel like a like a carnival show or something, you know, where you come in and there's a ride here, there's a ride here, and there's a show here, and there's a candy stand here, and there's all these things. You can't expect someone to just do it in order to go through and take this ride and then go over to the worldly gig and then go see the show with the bearded lady and then go buy cotton candy and things like that. A lot of people think that the step three, you know, step step one is you come into my world step two is you get this special free report, for example, or a DVD. And then step three, based on what you've learned in the video or the free report, then you can move on from there. That is often a stopping point. A lot of people really want to get going. They want to do it at their own pace and they want to own the free report. They like having the DVD or the video, but they don't want to consume it or they're not going to pay attention to it. One of the main mistakes a lot of marketers make is this very set down in stone path that you have to go through in order to get to the sale, and it just doesn't work that way. In fact, a lot of people buy stuff, will buy their second product from you before they've used or seen the first one. We had a client who used to send out, this is back in the 90s, they would send out actual videotapes, VHS. They had a series, a one, two, three series, you bought the first one, if you like that you'd buy the second one. And people were buying two and three, and then they found out that for six months they had been sending out blank VHS videos of the first step. So the people, anybody that watched it would have seen a blank tape and would have sent it back, but they were getting like almost no refunds, no requests, and yet people were still buying two and three because they wanted the product. So this was a good example of people often buying things and buying it was the big decision that they made, so it can sit on the shelf for a while before they get around to it. That's okay, and they still may want to take advantage of the other opportunities they have. They may want to join your group, they may want to have further contact with you, they may want to buy more of the same, they may want to get involved, but they haven't completely devoured that. And especially in any kind of funnel where you're trying to educate people to the point where they're going to feel good about buying, where the actual buying decision doesn't happen until down the line. Then if you're just putting obstacles in front of them, you've got to read this and you've got to understand this, and you've got to watch this and you've got to have this down, you're just asking for it. You're asking for people to drop out, to not participate. There's a little rebellious bit in people where they just say, no, I'm not going to do that. No matter how much they want it, no matter how much they believe your promise is, they will resist. So make it easy for people to go at their own pace, at their own rate, in their own peculiar way. Yeah, I'd say it's okay, let's just say you're going to have, you're doing a watch, and there's three videos or three emails. Good example. Email, in order to get understanding or value out of the second piece, don't make it a requirement to have watched or read or consume the first piece. People just won't do that. It's okay to open up with, hey, if you didn't read email number one, I recommend you first read the email I sent yesterday. Then go on, and everything else has to stand alone. It has to provide value, be understandable. This story must be its own pitch, and whatever the goal of it is, whatever the called action, if it's towards buying. Because it's just a common mistake we see where people, they think, well, if they really like my stuff, they'll do everything in my order. No one behaves that way. Your mother is the only one who will read every word you wrote in order. And even she won't remember what the first email was. So if the second email is dependent on me remembering what was in the first email, then you're really setting yourself up for a disaster. Because a lot of things are happening in people's lives. And even if the second email comes an hour later, a lot happened to your prospect in that hour. Okay, you talked about push funnels and pull funnels. There's always some follow-up questions. So let's just say your product is a left-handed monkey wrench. Let's talk about there's two, with push-pull, there's people that were searching, they went into a search engine, said, I need a left-handed monkey wrench. Or there's people who happen to be reading an article about tools. And then there was an ad about why you must have a left-handed monkey wrench in your toolbox or something. What happens next in their world, how or why might the copy be different? One letter isn't the letter for everybody. Sure. One sales page. Or one headline, for example. So like in monkey wrenches, for one, it would be why you need a monkey wrench. It's like a monkey wrench. Yes. Why you need a left-handed monkey wrench in your toolkit. That would be an example of pushing this. I didn't know monkey wrenches exist. The only left-handed ones. Oh my God, I need it after I've read your ad. The other one, the poll, would be here's the best monkey wrench you've ever owned. So two people who understand the value of monkey wrenches or left-handed monkey wrenches. And why this one now is a standout, for example. So it's the best left-handed monkey wrench you'll ever own. A completely different approach to selling the same product. Right. And so when you're working on your funnel, and if you don't have a current funnel, you'll build one first. But a common trip wire we see people is they want to write one website or one story and then send it to everybody. But I think that's a good example. I wanted to try to pull one out where why that frequently doesn't work, the opening. And much of the copy in the body, why yours might be the same. But it's often important to have, as you're working on thinking your funnels, if people are coming in with a different motivation, they came in to a different way, you may need to write different copy for them. Absolutely. In fact, we've toyed with having different option points very early on. If you're an entrepreneur, click here. If you're a copywriter, click here. If you're a rookie and you don't have a business yet, click here. And it takes you to different stages. Some people have done that with gender or with where you live in the country. If English is a second language, click here. If you live in the East Coast, click here. And there's a lot of things. And these are the way these kind of funnels kind of segment people to get them into the right spot. So writing one piece of the days of writing one piece of copy applies to everyone. We're in a lot of the copy you're having to say. So whether you're a rich man on the East Coast or a poor woman on the West Coast, and then it becomes bulky because you have to say that every time and you have to have two things. Just go ahead and divide that stuff up and then approach each person on their own ground and online. It's very, very simple and easy to do. Much easier than in print or in direct mail. Right, because you have more, the odds of you getting people to get to you by the way that you mapped out. You're never going to be 100%. But your odds are much, if you're putting an ad on the tool website, well, these people already know something about tools, you can write to them, to people differently than you. Oh my goodness, I need a tool. I never thought about that. Okay, the exercise this, I'll let John explain to you. Again, we're getting more down the line of putting what you're learning into context. Actually, writing more copy, thinking through the reason for each step. And then in the exercise, you're going to have a chance to start building out up or start designing what you're actually going to be writing and building. Okay, we've got an exercise for you for this part of the program. I want you to list the steps your prospect will go through from your first contact with him to the final sale or the final action, signing up. It could be very short, it could be three steps. He sees your banner ad somewhere, he clicks on it, goes to your website and opts in for a free newsletter. That could be the extent of your list. Or it could be you email him because he's on your email list, from there he goes to a site, he makes a decision, he goes somewhere, maybe there's a membership site, he signs up there, and then the payment plan goes out for six months or so. So just think about it, think about the steps. Think about what the little journey that your prospect is about to enter on. And the different points where he's going to have to make decisions, yeah, I'll do it or no, I've stopped. So think about that and write that down.
